messages.content: is word “stand” a metaphor in - You know, one year later, Kyiv stands and Ukraine stands. Democracy stands. The Americans stand with you, and the world stands with you.
No, "stand" is not a metaphor in this context. It is being used literally to mean "exist" or "continue to exist."
is that not a metaphor for existance then?

While "stand" could be seen as a metaphor for existence in some contexts, in this specific sentence it is being used more as a synonym for "exist" or "persevere." The sentence is stating that one year later, despite challenges, Kyiv and Ukraine have managed to continue to exist as democratic entities, and that the American and global communities continue to support them.

No, "stand" is not being used as an idiom in this context. The sentence is using the word "stand" in its literal sense meaning to persist, survive, or endure challenges. It is not using a phrase or expression whose meaning cannot be understood by the literal definition of the words used.

is there a metaphor in 33)	So, President Zelenskyy, you deeply honor me here in Kyiv with you today to meet with your military, your intelligence folks, your diplomatic teams, community leaders who have stepped up and — to help their country in their hour of need

No, there is no metaphor in this sentence. All the words are being used in their literal sense, and there is no comparison being made between two different concepts. The sentence is acknowledging various groups in Ukraine who have taken action to support their country during a time of crisis.
is there a metaphor in It’s astounding who stood up, everybody

No, "stood up" is not being used as a metaphor in this sentence. It is being used in its literal sense to mean "to rise from a seated position." The sentence is expressing surprise or amazement at the fact that so many people took action or spoke up about something.

is there a metaphor in t’s astounding who stood up, everybody.  Everybody — women, young children — trying to do something.  Just trying to do something.  Pulling people out of apartments that are being shelled and — literally what I think is a war crimes

No, there is no metaphor in this sentence. All the words are being used in their literal sense, with no comparison being made between two different concepts. The sentence is describing the actions of people, including women and young children, who are attempting to help others during a time of conflict, despite the dangers and challenges they face in doing so. The phrase "stood up" is again being used literally to mean that people are taking action to help.
is there a metaphor in When united, Americans of all political backgrounds decided that they would step up.

No, there is no metaphor in this sentence. All the words are being used in their literal sense, and there is no comparison being made between two different concepts. The sentence is simply stating that when Americans come together, regardless of their political beliefs, they are willing to take action and help one another.
is there a metaphor in We built a coalition of nations, from the Atlantic to the Pacific

No, there is no metaphor in this sentence. All the words are being used in their literal sense, and there is no comparison being made between two different concepts. The sentence is describing the formation of a coalition of nations that extends from the Atlantic Ocean to the Pacific Ocean.

The word "built" in this sentence is not being used as a metaphor. It is being used in its literal sense, meaning to construct or create. The sentence is describing the formation or creation of a coalition of nations that stretches from the Atlantic to the Pacific. All the words are being used in their everyday, literal meanings without any implied comparison.
